context.description: Reflective prose argues that self-perception drives an urge for internal consistency, even when it sustains harmful choices. It extends this idea to the way people judge themselves and others as evil, arguing that evil is usually projected onto others rather than recognized in oneself.
normalization_notes: Source title inferred from file name.

rows.text_content: Page 12 of 102 from the book Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2.

rows.paragraph_text: Internal consistency in self-perception recurs as a central theme.
rows.text_content: Actions, decisions, and words must align with self-image; otherwise mental and sometimes physical pain follows until a new consistency is restored.

rows.paragraph_text: A satirical fitness club example illustrates how people sustain contradictions.
rows.text_content: The text parodies postponed intentions and excuses to explain why members keep paying without acting.

rows.paragraph_text: The argument expands to investment, work, marriage, and the perception of evil.
rows.text_content: It claims that the same drive to avoid self-contradiction shapes moral judgment, making evil appear as something always located in others.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Book page from Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2. Philosophical reflection on self-perception, internal consistency, cognitive dissonance, and the projection of evil onto others. Page 12 discusses excuses, harmful commitments, and moral judgment.
